audit_status.source_base: Greek Galatians text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Jerusalem meeting, Titus not compelled, right hand of fellowship, Cephas confrontation, justification/making-right by trust/faithfulness, crucified with Messiah.

verses.restored: Then after fourteen years I went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, taking Titus along also.

I went up according to unveiling, and set before them the good news that I proclaim among the nations, privately to those seeming to be something, lest somehow I might be running or had run into emptiness.
But not even Titus, who was with me, being Greek, was compelled to be circumcised.
But because of false siblings secretly brought in, who slipped in to spy out our freedom that we have in Messiah Jesus, so that they might enslave us,
to whom we yielded in submission not even for an hour, so that the truth of the good news might remain with you.
But from those seeming to be something, whatever they once were makes no difference to me; the Divine takes no human face; for those seeming added nothing to me.
But on the contrary, seeing that I had been entrusted with the good news of the uncircumcision, just as Peter of the circumcision,
for the One working in Peter for apostleship of the circumcision worked also in me for the nations,

and knowing the grace given to me, James and Cephas and John, those seeming to be pillars, gave to me and Barnabas right hands of fellowship/participation, that we should go to the nations and they to the circumcision.
Only that we remember the poor, which very thing I was eager to do.
But when Cephas came to Antioch, I opposed him to his face, because he stood condemned.
For before certain ones came from James, he was eating with the nations; but when they came, he drew back and separated himself, fearing those from the circumcision.
And the rest of the Judeans joined him in hypocrisy, so that even Barnabas was carried away by their hypocrisy.

But when I saw that they were not walking straight toward the truth of the good news, I said to Cephas before all: If you, being Judean, live like the nations and not like Judean, how do you compel the nations to Judaize?
We, by nature Judeans and not sinners from the nations,

yet knowing that a human is not made righteous/just from works of Law but through trust/faithfulness of Jesus Messiah, even we trusted into Messiah Jesus, so that we might be made righteous/just from trust/faithfulness of Messiah and not from works of Law, because from works of Law no flesh will be made righteous/just.
But if, seeking to be made righteous/just in Messiah, we ourselves also were found sinners/misaligned, is Messiah then servant of sin/misalignment? May it not be.
For if what I tore down, these things I build again, I show myself a transgressor.
For through Law I died to Law, so that I might live to the Divine.

I have been crucified with Messiah; and I no longer live, but Messiah lives in me. And what I now live in flesh, I live in trust, the trust/faithfulness of the Son of the Divine, who loved me and gave himself for me.
I do not set aside the grace of the Divine; for if righteousness/justice is through Law, then Messiah died freely/for nothing.

translation_notes: Works of Law should be read in the Galatians context as boundary/covenant practices pressed on nations, not as a generic attack on all good works.
Pistis Christou in verses 16 and 20 is preserved with trust/faithfulness ambiguity, allowing both Messiah's faithfulness and human trust-response.
Cephas confrontation is an intra-apostolic dispute over table fellowship and Gentile inclusion, not anti-Jewish polemic.
